title: Emergence of spin entanglement with the pseudogap onset in the Fermi-Hubbard model
authors: Frederic Bippus, Thomas Chalopin, Gabriele Bellomia, Gergő Roósz, Titus Franz, Antoine Georges, Anna Kauch, Immanuel Bloch, Karsten Held
abstract: Despite decades of intense theoretical and experimental investigation, the two-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model still resists a complete microscopic understanding. Conventional approaches typically probe global observables and locally resolved correlation functions. Here, we develop a complementary perspective based on the measurement of entanglement. Using both an ultracold-atom quantum simulator and numerical simulations based on the dynamical vertex approximation, we find that entanglement is closely tied to the onset of the enigmatic pseudogap regime: spin-singlet entanglement emerges only as the pseudogap sets in and, in contrast to classical correlations, remains confined to nearest-neighbour sites in this regime. Our results, therefore, disfavour purely classical-fluctuation theories of the pseudogap and constrain microscopic models to those that develop nearest-neighbour spin-singlet entanglement at the pseudogap onset.
summary: This work investigates the Fermi-Hubbard model by measuring quantum entanglement, finding that spin-singlet entanglement appears only when the pseudogap opens. This result strongly suggests that the pseudogap physics requires genuine quantum correlations localized at nearest neighbors, ruling out purely classical explanations.
main_problem: To gain a microscopic understanding of the two-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model, the authors move beyond traditional observables by measuring quantum entanglement.
main_result: They find that spin-singlet entanglement emerges precisely at the onset of the pseudogap regime and is strictly confined to nearest-neighbor sites, disfavoring purely classical fluctuation theories.
method: The study combines ultracold-atom quantum simulation experiments with advanced numerical calculations using the dynamical vertex approximation (DΓA).
model_or_system: The system is the two-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model, simulated using ultracold atoms in an optical lattice, and analyzed theoretically via the DΓA.
key_observables: SSR negativity (entanglement), spin-singlet/spin-triplet ratio, and mutual information ($I$).
important_parameters: Pseudogap onset, nearest-neighbor distance, doping ($\delta$), and temperature ($T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Superselection Rules (SSR) to restrict entanglement measurements to operationally accessible components.
figures_summary: Figures compare the nearest-neighbor SSR negativity (experiment vs. theory) across temperature and doping, and show the spatial structure of mutual information.
paper_structure: The paper develops the entanglement diagnostic, applies it to the Fermi-Hubbard model across varying parameters, and contrasts the short-range quantum entanglement with longer-range classical correlations.
why_interesting: The direct experimental measurement of entanglement in a clean quantum simulator platform provides a powerful, non-equilibrium diagnostic tool to distinguish between quantum and classical physics in strongly correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
